hypothesis: Incorporating stereochemical diversity metrics—specifically tracking chiral center distribution and enantiomeric ratios across generated molecules—into NaviDiv's constraint framework will reveal that current reinforcement learning optimization disproportionately collapses stereochemical space before structural fingerprint-based metrics detect diversity loss, and that adding stereochemistry-aware penalties will recover a measurable population of high-scoring chiral candidates that are systematically excluded by optimization runs using only the currently implemented achiral Morgan fingerprint constraints.
